species.headline: The faucet and the first toll-gate: the only organism that mints new joules, by tapping the abiotic light plane.
species.taxonomy: A deliberately abstract stand-in for any photoautotroph (terrestrial plants, algae). The default species (data/species/default.json), 9 decoupled morphology traits. No real NCBITaxon node yet — the schematic producer.
species.ontology: Loci tagged SO:0000704 (gene) with abstract GO function refs (GO:0009579 thylakoid foliage, GO:0003006 reproduction). The load-bearing lever is LeafSize, driving the Acquisition budget — a bigger leaf is a bigger solar panel.
species.phenology: The renewable clock: its light-driven Net Primary Production is minted every tick, the slow background pulse every downstream boom/bust is a phase-shifted echo of.
species.famous_fact: A producer never hands all its captured energy up the chain — only the leftover Net Primary Production reaches consumers. Photosynthesis both feeds every food web AND oxygenated the atmosphere (the Great Oxidation Event).
species.sim_role: Autotroph — income from the light channel only (the solar-influx proxy).

species.headline: Biology's lab rat, re-cast as the recycler — a metabolic generalist that mineralizes detritus back into free nutrient.
species.taxonomy: E. coli str. K-12 substr. MG1655, the field's reference E. coli. The K-12 lineage was isolated in 1922 and cultured for a century — the single most-studied free-living organism on Earth. NCBITaxon:511145.
species.ontology: 136 GO-MF-curated loci (BiGG e_coli_core × CDS GCF_000005845.2). The 5 anchor genes span the four carbon-flux verbs: transport-and-phosphorylate (ptsG), condense-into-TCA (gltA), ferment-to-acetyl-CoA (pflB), overflow-to-acetate (pta), reduce-to-lactate (ldhA).
species.phenology: A facultative anaerobe whose life cycle is metabolic mode-switching: lag → exponential (~20 min doubling) → stationary → death. Above a critical growth rate (~0.27 /h) it flips into overflow metabolism, spilling acetate via Pta-AckA even with oxygen.
species.famous_fact: The most-sequenced genome in history was still a work in progress: the 1997 Science deposit carried 243 sequencing errors, corrected only in the curated U00096.3.
species.sim_role: Decomposer — mineralizes detritus → free nutrient, minus the respired efficiency tax. Eligible prey for the predator.

species.headline: A comma-shaped bacterium that hunts and eats other bacteria — from the inside. The sim's apex predator.
species.taxonomy: Genus Bdellovibrio (Greek bdella, 'leech' + Latin vibrio, 'comma-shaped'); bacteriovorus = 'bacteria-devouring'. Phylum Bdellovibrionota. Reference strain HD100 (NCBITaxon:264462) — the first predatory bacterium ever fully sequenced. One of the smallest free-living bacteria known.
species.ontology: A genome packed with paralogous degradative-enzyme families — proteases, nucleases, lipases, peptidoglycan hydrolases. The sim anchors PredationCapacity on GO:0008745 (lysozyme / peptidoglycan-muralytic activity) — the hit/lytic attack lever.
species.phenology: A 'Jekyll-and-Hyde' biphasic life cycle (~3-4 h). Attack phase: a free-swimming, non-replicating hunter, the fastest-swimming bacterium relative to its size (~160 µm/s) on one sheathed polar flagellum. Entry: it gnaws into the prey periplasm and rounds the host into a sealed bdelloplast. Growth phase: it grows as a single filament, then septates into 3-6 progeny at once.
species.famous_fact: The textbook 'living antibiotic': because it eats prey rather than poisoning one molecular target, prey can't easily evolve resistance. It spares Gram-positive bacteria and human cells — self-limiting.
species.sim_role: Predator — income from predation only, taps no abiotic channel. Preys on Heterotroph + Decomposer (E. coli), spares Autotroph and Predator. Not itself prey.

species.headline: The wall-less minimalist — one of the smallest genomes and smallest free-living cells, a soft pleomorphic blob with no membrane to brace it.
species.taxonomy: Class Mollicutes ('soft skin'), genus Mycoplasma. M. genitalium has ~580 kb and ~525 genes — long the benchmark for the smallest self-replicating genome of a free-living organism. NCBITaxon:2097. No peptidoglycan cell wall (hence wall-less and antibiotic-distinct).
species.ontology: A stripped core-machinery genome — translation (tuf/EF-Tu), chaperones (dnaK/groL), RNA polymerase (rpoC) — with nearly every dispensable pathway deleted. The retained set IS the story: this is close to the irreducible minimum of cellular life.
species.phenology: Slow, fastidious growth; an obligate parasite/commensal of mucosal surfaces that scavenges nutrients it can no longer make. Pleomorphic (shape-shifting) because it lacks the rigid wall that gives other bacteria a fixed form.
species.famous_fact: Mycoplasma genitalium's genome was the template for the JCVI minimal-cell project — its gene set defined what 'essential for life' even means, and inspired Syn3.0.
species.sim_role: Heterotroph — draws organic-carbon taps. Wall-less and small. Eligible prey.

species.headline: The Gram-positive sporulation model — and Bdellovibrio's uneatable prey. When starved, it builds a near-indestructible endospore.
species.taxonomy: Phylum Bacillota (Firmicutes), genus Bacillus. B. subtilis str. 168 is the model Gram-positive bacterium, the workhorse of bacterial genetics and developmental biology. NCBITaxon:1423. Thick Gram-positive cell wall — which is exactly why Bdellovibrio cannot prey on it.
species.ontology: The sporulation cascade is the marquee circuit: the master regulator spo0A triggers an asymmetric division, then the compartment-specific sigma factors sigF → sigE → sigG → sigK drive endospore maturation (sspA/sspB coat the DNA, cotE builds the coat, gerAA germinates it). SporulationCapacity reads this cascade.
species.phenology: A vegetative rod that, on nutrient exhaustion, commits to sporulation: an asymmetric septum traps a forespore, the mother cell engulfs and wraps it in coats, then lyses — releasing a dormant endospore that can survive heat, radiation, and centuries of starvation, germinating when food returns.
species.famous_fact: Bacillus endospores are the gold standard of dormancy — viable spores have been revived from amber and salt crystals tens of millions of years old. You cannot sterilize a spore-former by ordinary means.
species.sim_role: Decomposer (sim role) with a spore-former marker — extracellular digestion, plus the dormancy/germination mechanic. Gram-positive: Bdellovibrio-immune. Eligible prey only when vegetative.

species.headline: The biofilm engineer — a metabolic versatilist that builds a sheltering matrix slime and swims on a single polar flagellum.
species.taxonomy: Phylum Pseudomonadota (Gammaproteobacteria), genus Pseudomonas. P. aeruginosa is a ubiquitous environmental opportunist with an unusually large, plastic genome encoding hundreds of catabolic pathways. NCBITaxon:287. A notorious nosocomial pathogen.
species.ontology: A genome rich in regulatory and transport genes — the wide metabolic repertoire that lets it eat almost anything. The biofilm program (extracellular polymeric substance, quorum sensing, polar flagellum + type IV pili motility) is its defining lifestyle circuit.
species.phenology: Switches between a free-swimming planktonic state and a sessile biofilm: cells aggregate, secrete an EPS matrix, and coordinate via quorum sensing into a structured, drug-tolerant community. The matrix halo is its signature — a chemical fortress.
species.famous_fact: Pseudomonas biofilms are up to 1000× more antibiotic-tolerant than planktonic cells — the matrix is a physical and chemical shield, the reason P. aeruginosa infections are so stubborn.
species.sim_role: Mixotroph — blends abiotic and organic taps. Builds a biofilm matrix; polar-flagellated. Eligible prey when planktonic.

species.headline: The grape-cluster of the skin: non-motile cocci that divide in multiple planes and pile up into irregular bunches.
species.taxonomy: Phylum Bacillota, genus Staphylococcus (Greek staphyle, 'bunch of grapes'). S. aureus is a Gram-positive commensal and pathogen of skin and nares. NCBITaxon:1280. Famous as MRSA when methicillin-resistant.
species.ontology: A coccoid (spherical) Gram-positive that divides in alternating planes without separating, producing the characteristic 3D grape-cluster. Non-motile (no flagella). The genome carries a versatile virulence and resistance arsenal.
species.phenology: Skin and mucosal flora most of the time; an opportunistic invader given a breach. Forms clusters because daughter cells stay attached after division in multiple planes — a packing geometry, not a colony structure.
species.famous_fact: Staphylococcus aureus is the textbook grape-cluster coccus — its name literally means 'golden bunch of grapes', for the cluster shape and the golden carotenoid (staphyloxanthin) that shields it from immune oxidants.
species.sim_role: Heterotroph — draws organic-carbon taps. Coccoid, non-motile. Eligible prey.

species.headline: The quiet skin commensal — a short, non-motile rod that lives in the dim anaerobic depths of the sebaceous follicle.
species.taxonomy: Phylum Actinomycetota, genus Cutibacterium (formerly Propionibacterium). C. acnes is the dominant resident of human sebaceous skin, slow-growing and aerotolerant-anaerobic. NCBITaxon:1747. Implicated in acne and in low-grade implant infections.
species.ontology: A propionic-acid-fermenting genome tuned for lipid-rich, oxygen-poor follicles — it digests sebum and ferments to short-chain fatty acids. Short rod morphology, no flagella (non-motile).
species.phenology: A stable, slow-turnover member of the skin microbiome that thrives in the anaerobic sebaceous gland. Its fermentation acidifies the follicle; population swings track sebum supply, not motility.
species.famous_fact: Cutibacterium acnes is one of the most abundant organisms on healthy human skin — and its propionic-acid fermentation helps keep skin pH acidic, a built-in defense against transient pathogens.
species.sim_role: Decomposer (sim role) — extracellular digestion of lipid/dead matter → free nutrient. Short non-motile rod. Eligible prey.

species.headline: The black mold — branching hyphae topped by a globose vesicle radiating biseriate conidia chains, the famous black A. niger head.
species.taxonomy: Kingdom Fungi, phylum Ascomycota, genus Aspergillus. A. niger is a ubiquitous black-spored mold and an industrial workhorse (citric-acid and enzyme production). NCBITaxon:5061. A filamentous fungus, not a bacterium.
species.ontology: The conidiation cascade is the headline circuit: fluG/flbA upstream signaling activates the central regulator brlA → abaA → wetA, which builds the asexual fruiting structure — a conidiophore stalk, a swollen vesicle, metulae+phialides, and chains of dark conidia. SporulationCapacity reads this cascade.
species.phenology: Grows as a mycelium of branching hyphae; on nutrient or surface cues it raises aerial conidiophores that bloom into the globose, black-pigmented conidial head, releasing airborne spores. Growth rate drives hyphal extent; the brlA cascade drives the head density.
species.famous_fact: Aspergillus niger is the industrial source of most of the world's citric acid — fermented at megaton scale — and its black conidial heads are the classic 'bread mold' silhouette of mycology textbooks.
species.sim_role: Decomposer (sim role) — extracellular digestion → free nutrient, via a mold morphology (hyphae + conidiophore). Eligible prey.

species.headline: The brush mold — branching hyphae topped by the penicillus, a brush-like cluster of conidia chains, and the source of penicillin.
species.taxonomy: Kingdom Fungi, phylum Ascomycota, genus Penicillium (Latin penicillus, 'painter's brush'). P. chrysogenum is the original penicillin producer. NCBITaxon:5076. A filamentous fungus.
species.ontology: The same fluG/flbA → brlA → abaA → wetA conidiation cascade as Aspergillus, but the head architecture differs: instead of a swollen vesicle it branches into a brush (the penicillus) of metulae, phialides, and conidial chains. SporulationCapacity reads the cascade; the head form is the brush variant.
species.phenology: A fast-growing blue-green mold of soil and decaying matter; raises brush-shaped conidiophores that shed chains of conidia. Growth rate drives hyphal extent; the cascade drives the brush density.
species.famous_fact: A Penicillium mold contaminating Alexander Fleming's plate in 1928 cleared the surrounding Staphylococcus — the accidental discovery of penicillin, the first antibiotic and the start of the antibiotic era.
species.sim_role: Decomposer (sim role) — extracellular digestion → free nutrient, via a mold morphology (hyphae + brush conidiophore). Eligible prey.

species.headline: The most reduced cellular genome — ~160 kb of barely-cell, living inside a psyllid bacteriocyte, trading amino acids for shelter.
species.taxonomy: An obligate endosymbiont (Gammaproteobacteria) of psyllids (plant-sap insects), housed inside specialized host cells called bacteriocytes. NCBITaxon:114186. Carsonella ruddii's genome (~160 kb, ~182 genes) is among the smallest of any cellular organism — so reduced that it blurs the line between bacterium and organelle.
species.ontology: Nearly nothing remains but translation machinery (tuf/EF-Tu, fusA, ribosomal proteins) and a few retained biosynthesis genes for the amino acids its host cannot make (the aromatic-amino-acid and leucine pathways: leuB/leuD, aroB/aroC/aroK). SymbiosisCapacity reads this host-provisioning exchange.
species.phenology: Cannot live outside the host — it is vertically transmitted from mother to egg, never free-living. Its entire 'life cycle' is the bacteriocyte: it provisions the psyllid with essential amino acids missing from plant sap, and the host supplies everything else.
species.famous_fact: Carsonella ruddii has lost so many genes — even ones thought universally essential — that biologists debate whether it is still a living cell or has become an organelle in the making.
species.sim_role: ObligateSymbiont — no abiotic tap of its own; income via the host-coupling exchange. Lives inside a host cell. Not free-living.

species.headline: The minimal cell — a human-designed synthetic genome of only the genes deemed essential to live and divide. Life, stripped to the studs.
species.taxonomy: JCVI-syn3.0 is a synthetic bacterium built by the J. Craig Venter Institute from a Mycoplasma chassis, carrying just 473 genes (~531 kb) — the smallest genome of any autonomously replicating cell. Not a wild taxon: a designed minimal organism.
species.ontology: Only the irreducible core: the translation apparatus (tuf, fusA, ribosomal proteins, rpoB/rpoC), DNA replication (polC, dnaK), and a stripped glycolysis (fba, gap, eno, pyk, tpiA, ptsG) for energy. A retained glyA exchange anchors SymbiosisCapacity. Astonishingly, ~149 of its essential genes have NO known function.
species.phenology: Grows and divides in rich medium, but slowly and with bizarre pleomorphic morphology — removing the 'non-essential' genes that tidy up division left it shape-irregular. A blank-slate chassis for synthetic biology.
species.famous_fact: JCVI-syn3.0 was announced in 2016 as the minimal genome — and its biggest surprise was humbling: about a third of its essential genes are of completely unknown function. We built a living cell we don't fully understand.
species.sim_role: ObligateSymbiont (sim role) — a chassis-minimal cell; income via the host-coupling/exchange seam. Not free-living in the open economy.

genes.ontology: EC 2.3.3.1; acetyl-CoA + oxaloacetate → citrate. The first committed, rate-setting step of the TCA (Krebs) cycle.
genes.famous_fact: >62% of all acetyl-CoA flux passes through citrate synthase during aerobic growth on glucose — the single largest native acetyl-CoA drain.
genes.knockdown: Chokes full oxidation → carbon backs up as acetyl-CoA and spills into overflow (acetate).

genes.ontology: The PTS permease (Enzyme IICB-Glc): imports glucose AND phosphorylates it in one move via a PEP→EI→HPr→EIIA→EIICB relay.
genes.famous_fact: The hub of carbon-catabolite repression — how the cell senses glucose and decides which sugar to eat first. The solute is chemically modified during transport (group translocation).
genes.knockdown: Throttle ptsG and you starve the whole downstream network of carbon — the literal intake valve.

genes.ontology: EC 2.3.1.54; pyruvate + CoA → acetyl-CoA + formate. A glycyl-radical enzyme that cleaves the C–C bond without producing reducing equivalents.
genes.famous_fact: That radical is so reactive that oxygen destroys it — O2 exposure cleaves the protein chain — so PFL is THE anaerobic route to acetyl-CoA, active only when oxygen is gone.
genes.knockdown: An anaerobic cell loses its main way to make acetyl-CoA from pyruvate, forcing carbon toward lactate (ldhA).

genes.ontology: EC 2.3.1.8; acetyl-CoA + phosphate ⇌ acetyl-phosphate + CoA. The first committed step of the Pta-AckA pathway converting acetyl-CoA to acetate while harvesting an ATP.
genes.famous_fact: The engine of overflow metabolism (the bacterial Crabtree effect): fed sugar faster than the TCA cycle can burn it, E. coli just dumps the excess as acetate for quick ATP.
genes.knockdown: Block acetate overflow → carbon pushes back into efficient oxidation. In the sim it gene-drives the decomposer's mineralization rate.

genes.ontology: (R)-lactate + NAD+ ⇌ pyruvate + NADH + H+. Fermentative — reduces pyruvate to D-lactate using NADH.
genes.famous_fact: Its real job isn't the lactate — it's the NAD+: anaerobic glycolysis keeps consuming NAD+, and ldhA regenerates it to keep ATP flowing. High Km, so it only fires when pyruvate accumulates.
genes.knockdown: An anaerobic cell loses a main way to dump excess electrons — pairs naturally against pflB at the same pyruvate fork.

genes.ontology: GO:0008745 — peptidoglycan hydrolase / muralytic activity: the enzymes that breach the prey cell wall during entry and digestion.
genes.famous_fact: Bdellovibrio carries dozens of paralogous peptidoglycan hydrolases — the molecular toolkit that lets it gnaw into the periplasm, reseal the hole, and remodel the host into a bdelloplast.
genes.knockdown: A hit-locus CRISPRi knockdown (PredationCapacity→0) throttles the attack — the OVERSIGHT lever that disarms the predator.

genes.ontology: Spo0A is the phosphorelay-activated master transcription factor that, when starvation drives its phosphorylation past threshold, commits the cell to asymmetric division and the endospore program.
genes.famous_fact: Spo0A integrates the cell's nutritional and quorum signals into a single yes/no decision — the most-studied bacterial developmental switch, a bistable circuit that gambles the cell's whole future on dormancy.
genes.knockdown: Knock spo0A down and the cell cannot commit to sporulation — it stays vegetative and loses its dormancy escape hatch.

genes.ontology: SigF is the first compartment-specific sigma factor activated in the forespore after asymmetric septation, launching the cascade sigF → sigE → sigG → sigK that builds the endospore.
genes.famous_fact: SigF is held inactive by an anti-sigma factor until the very moment of asymmetric division — a molecular timer that fires sporulation in exactly the right compartment.
genes.knockdown: Disrupt sigF and the forespore-side cascade stalls — the endospore never matures.

genes.ontology: BrlA ('bristle') is the central regulator of asexual development in Aspergillus: it sits between upstream signaling (fluG/flbA) and the downstream abaA → wetA cascade that builds the conidiophore and conidial chains.
genes.famous_fact: A brlA loss-of-function mutant grows endless bristle-like stalks that never form a conidial head — the phenotype that named the gene and pinned it as the conidiation master switch.
genes.knockdown: Knock brlA down and the mold makes hyphae but no spore-bearing head — it loses asexual reproduction.

genes.ontology: AbaA ('abacus') acts downstream of brlA to drive phialide differentiation — the spore-producing cells of the conidiophore — before wetA matures the spores.
genes.famous_fact: An abaA mutant produces beaded, abacus-like chains of misformed cells instead of proper phialides — the visual that named the gene.
genes.knockdown: Knock abaA down and the head forms but cannot make proper spore-bearing phialides.

genes.ontology: GO:0006414 — tuf encodes elongation factor Tu (EF-Tu), which delivers aminoacyl-tRNAs to the ribosome. One of the last genes any reduced genome gives up — translation is non-negotiable.
genes.famous_fact: EF-Tu is among the most abundant proteins in a bacterial cell and one of the most conserved across all life — so central that even the ~160 kb Carsonella genome keeps it.
genes.knockdown: Throttle tuf and you throttle protein synthesis itself — the retained growth backbone of a minimal cell.

genes.ontology: GO:0008652 — a retained amino-acid biosynthesis gene. Carsonella keeps just the pathways (leucine, aromatic amino acids) for the nutrients its psyllid host cannot make from plant sap.
genes.famous_fact: The symbiont's entire reason to exist: it provisions the host with essential amino acids missing from a sap diet, and in exchange the host supplies everything the symbiont has stopped making.
genes.knockdown: Knock the provisioning gene down (SymbiosisCapacity→0) and the host-coupling exchange collapses — the symbiont stops paying its rent.

roles.title: Autotroph (self-feeding)
roles.one_line: Fixes inorganic carbon using an external energy source — the base of every chain.
roles.body: Photoautotrophs draw from sunlight, chemo-/lithoautotrophs from oxidizing inorganic compounds. In the sim it taps the light channel only.

roles.title: Heterotroph (other-feeding)
roles.one_line: Cannot fix carbon; must eat organic carbon others made.
roles.body: Every animal, fungus, and most bacteria. In the sim it draws organic-carbon taps (free_nutrient/detritus). Eligible prey.

roles.title: Mixotroph
roles.one_line: Blends or switches between autotrophy and heterotrophy.
roles.body: The in-betweener — phagotrophic algae form a continuum of nutritional modes; can photosynthesize AND eat at once. A living rebuttal to tidy textbook categories.

roles.title: Decomposer (saprotroph)
roles.one_line: Extracellular digestion of dead matter → free nutrient. Eligible prey.
roles.body: A chemoheterotroph using extracellular digestion — it secretes enzymes into the environment, then absorbs the freed nutrients. Nature's recycler; why an ecosystem isn't buried in its own corpses. In the sim: E. coli and the soil microbes, mineralizing detritus → free nutrient.

roles.title: Predator
roles.one_line: Earns only by consuming other organisms' joules — taps no abiotic channel.
roles.body: A strict predator (that structural purity is why it's a dedicated role, not 'a hungry heterotroph'). In the sim: Bdellovibrio, eating the E. coli decomposer. Not itself prey.

roles.title: Obligate symbiont
roles.one_line: Lives inside a host, trading a provisioned product for shelter — not free-living.
roles.body: An obligate endosymbiont with a reduced genome that has outsourced most metabolism to its host. In the sim it has no abiotic tap of its own; income comes via the host-coupling exchange (SymbiosisCapacity).

flows.title: Solar light influx
flows.phenology: Primary production / photosynthesis — the only joules minted into the world each tick.
flows.body: NPP is what's left after the producer's own respiration, and it is the entire ecosystem's energy budget; every downstream flow is a transformation of these minted joules, never a creation of new ones.

flows.title: Decomposer mineralization
flows.phenology: Detritus → free nutrient — the soil nutrient cycle that closes the loop.
flows.body: Dead organic matter is converted into plant-available inorganic nutrient, the rest respired as the efficiency tax. The plant's own dead matter feeds the microbe, which regenerates the nutrient the plant needs.

flows.title: Predation
flows.body: A predator consumes a co-located prey's joules on a frozen snapshot — it gains energy, pays an efficiency tax, and the carcass returns to detritus. Remove the predator and watch the cascade ripple to the plant.

flows.title: Chemical / allelopathy
flows.phenology: The toxin field — microbial chemical warfare.
flows.body: Organisms spend joules to emit into three planes — toxin (allelopathy), kin (a presence marker), and alarm (distress) — which diffuse, decay, and are sensed by neighbours. Real allelopathy is a community-level negotiation, not one-way poisoning.
